Toxic — the sap and seeds contain diterpene esters. One of the more reliably toxic landscape plants in Florida.
origin: Non-native. Native to Malaysia, Indonesia, and the western Pacific islands. Member of Euphorbiaceae — the spurge family, same as Copperleaf, Chenille Plant, and Coral Plant in the collection.
more_information: Crotons are scattered throughout the park, most donated from volunteers' home gardens to anchor areas with year-round color. Near the gazebo, an informal croton showcase has developed — plants of all shapes, sizes, and leaf varieties on display together, a good spot to appreciate how much variety exists within a single species.
A June 2026 addition fronts the left side of the cactus garden, brought and planted by a volunteer from Sarasota.
wildlife_value: Limited direct wildlife value. The flowers are inconspicuous. The toxic sap deters browsing by most animals.
Birds occasionally perch in the dense foliage. The primary ecological role in a Florida landscape is ornamental rather than wildlife-functional.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Inconspicuous, small, white, in spikes above the foliage. Not ornamentally significant.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: The display. Extremely variable — leathery, in shapes from oval to strap-like to lobed. Colors include combinations of green, yellow, orange, red, and purple, with patterns from solid to blotched to veined. No two cultivars look alike.

blocks.label: Sap
blocks.text: Milky white latex when stems or leaves are broken.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Upright evergreen shrub, 3 to 8 feet. Dense, colorful.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: The multicolored leaves are unmistakable — no other common Florida shrub produces this color range on a single leaf. The milky sap from broken stems confirms euphorbia family membership.

size.habit: erect, bushy evergreen shrub
size.height: typically 3 to 8 feet in the landscape; can reach about 10 feet

growing_conditions.light: bright light to full sun brings out the strongest leaf color; tolerates part shade with more muted color
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: moist, well-drained, fertile
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-tender; damaged below about 40°F; USDA zones 10 to 11 (9B with protection)
growing_conditions.note: color is best in strong light; leaf drop follows cold snaps or sudden moves. Sap stains and irritates skin.

detail: Not edible. All parts toxic.

people: Toxic. Contains diterpene esters in sap and seeds. Ingestion causes vomiting, diarrhea, and burning sensation in mouth and throat. Sap causes skin and eye irritation. Wash hands after handling.
dogs: Toxic to dogs and cats. Ingestion causes drooling, vomiting, and diarrhea. Sap is a skin irritant. Keep pets away.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive plant list. Not flagged by UF/IFAS.

safety_note: Do not eat any part of this plant.
Its sap and seeds contain diterpene esters that cause vomiting, diarrhea, and a burning sensation in the mouth and throat if swallowed, and the sap irritates skin and eyes.
Wash your hands after handling it.
It's toxic to dogs and cats as well, causing drooling, vomiting, and diarrhea. Keep pets away.

quick_hits: Low in the middle of Bird of Paradise Island, beneath the towering birds, sits the most historically precious plant in the park: an Amazon Lily handed down through the Baden family since the 1880s.
It has survived nearly a century and a half the way heirloom plants do — cutting after cutting, bulb division after bulb division, passed from one generation to the next.
Hurricane saltwater recently flooded it down to a few bare stubs. It refused to quit, and is pushing back up again — a fragrant white-flowered Amazonian forest bulb that simply will not die.
Look for the placard that tells its story, tucked among the giant Bird of Paradise, a Yellow Elder, and a Geiger tree.
origin: Native to the shaded, humid forest floors of the western Amazon basin — Peru and Colombia, in the foothills of the Andes. A bulb of the amaryllis family adapted to deep dappled shade, which is why it thrives low and protected here on Bird of Paradise Island rather than out in the open sun.
more_information: The Amazon Lily is Palma Sola's living heirloom. The specimen low in the center of Bird of Paradise Island has been in the Baden family since the 1880s, kept alive across roughly a century and a half not as a single plant but as an unbroken chain of cuttings and bulb divisions — each generation lifting offsets from the last and replanting them.

The plant you see is, in a real sense, the same plant a Baden tended before Florida was crisscrossed with highways. Nick Baden, one of the park's founders and now in his nineties, still comes to work; among other things he runs the tractor to gather the debris piles, secures the permits, and personally babysits the bonfires that keep the grounds clear.
The lily is his family's thread running straight through the park's history.

Botanically it's a wonderful match for that role. Eucharis is a shade-loving bulb from the Amazonian forest floor, and like many bulbs it reproduces by offsets — small daughter bulbs that cluster around the parent — so it is almost made to be divided and shared.

That same underground bulb is why it's so hard to kill: when hurricane saltwater flooded the island and burned the foliage down to a few bare stubs, the bulbs held on below ground and began pushing up new growth. Salt-scorched to nubs, it still won't quit.

When it's happy and in bloom, the reward is a cluster of pure white, fragrant, daffodil-like flowers held above broad, glossy, deep-green leaves — each bloom a ring of six tepals around a green-tinged central cup, sweetly scented like jasmine. It earns its other name, the Eucharist Lily, from that luminous white.

Its home on Bird of Paradise Island is a fine piece of park theater in itself: some twenty massive Bird of Paradise plants tower over it, with a Yellow Elder and a Geiger tree sharing the bed, and the small Amazon Lily and its storied placard set low in the middle — easy to walk past, impossible to forget once you know what it is.
wildlife_value: The Amazon Lily's fragrant white flowers can draw the occasional bee or butterfly, but it offers little nectar and isn't a significant wildlife plant in Florida — its tubular, scent-forward flowers are built for specific tropical pollinators back in its Amazonian home.

Its value here is human and historical rather than ecological: a deep-shade groundcover bulb that anchors the understory of Bird of Paradise Island and carries 140 years of the park's family history. Most animals leave the bulb and foliage alone.

blocks.label: Reproduces readily by offsets
blocks.text: daughter bulbs that form around the parent — which is exactly how it has been propagated and passed down here for generations. Also grown from seed, but division is the traditional method. Slow, reliable, and tolerant of crowding (it actually blooms better slightly rootbound).

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Clusters of 3 to 6 pure white, fragrant flowers on a leafless stalk above the foliage; each flower has six spreading tepals around a green-tinged central staminal cup, daffodil-like. Sweetly scented. Blooms one or more times a year, often in the cooler months.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Broad, glossy, dark green, ribbed, somewhat like peace-lily or hosta foliage, rising from the bulb.

blocks.label: Habit
blocks.text: Clumping, evergreen, shade-loving bulb forming tight rosettes.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: On Bird of Paradise Island, look LOW and to the middle, beneath the giant Bird of Paradise and near the Yellow Elder and Geiger tree. Find the historical placard — that's your marker. Right now the lily may look like little more than a few stubs recovering from salt damage; that humble appearance is part of the story. In better seasons, watch for the broad glossy leaves and the fragrant white flower clusters held above them.

size.height: 1 to 2 feet of foliage; flower stalks rise 18-24 inches above the leaves
size.spread: clumping, slowly expanding by offset bulbs
size.habit: evergreen shade bulb in tight rosettes
size.texture: bold, glossy foliage; elegant white blooms

growing_conditions.light: dappled to deep shade (protect from harsh afternoon sun, which scorches leaves)
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: rich, moist, well-drained, organic
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: moderate once established; prefers consistent moisture
growing_conditions.salt_tolerance: low (recently salt-flooded here and knocked back to stubs)
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: frost-tender; USDA zones 10-11 outdoors
growing_conditions.note: blooms better slightly crowded/rootbound; propagate and share by dividing offsets — the traditional method that has kept this specimen alive since the 1880s

detail: Not edible. Like other amaryllis-family bulbs, it contains alkaloids and should not be eaten.

people: Mildly toxic. As a member of the amaryllis family, the bulb and foliage contain alkaloids (lycorine-type) that can cause nausea and vomiting if ingested. Not a serious hazard in a garden setting, but not for eating.
dogs: Mildly toxic to dogs. Amaryllis-family bulbs can cause drooling, vomiting, and digestive upset if chewed or eaten — the bulb is the most concentrated part. Discourage digging and chewing.

invasive.notes: Not on Florida's FISC invasive lists. A slow, clumping shade bulb; no spread concern.

internal_notes: PARK CONTEXT (Randy) — SHOWCASE/HERITAGE PLANT: Located low in the center of Bird of Paradise Island, beneath ~20 massive Bird of Paradise plants; bed also holds a Yellow Elder and a Geiger tree. In the Baden family since the 1880s, propagated cutting/division after division across generations.

Recently salt-flooded by hurricane storm surge down to bare stubs but resprouting — 'it won't quit.' Nick Baden, a park founder, in his 90s, still works — runs the tractor to build debris piles, gets authority permits, and babysits the bonfire burns himself. There is a historical placard at the plant telling its story.

TO DO: Randy to photograph the placard. SPECIES TO VERIFY from placard — Amazon Lily may be Eucharis amazonica or the hybrid Eucharis x grandiflora (both share the common name). Sign Level set to Genus until confirmed.
INTERPRETIVE GOLD: Arguably the park's most story-rich specimen — heirloom continuity + founder + resilience. Strong candidate for featured signage and web storytelling.
CONSENT: Confirm Nick Baden is comfortable being named on public signage/web before publishing.
safety_note: Don't eat any part of this plant. Like other amaryllis-family bulbs it contains lycorine-type alkaloids — most concentrated in the bulb — that can cause nausea and vomiting.
It's not a serious hazard in the garden, but it isn't for eating.
It's mildly toxic to dogs as well; chewing the bulb or foliage can cause drooling, vomiting, and digestive upset. Discourage digging and chewing.

quick_hits: A Florida native shrub or small tree that earns its keep year-round. In late winter and early spring it covers itself in small white flower clusters, locally nicknamed Florida snow, that draw bees and butterflies.
The red-to-black drupes that follow are eaten by birds and other wildlife, and the dense, fine foliage gives songbirds, including cardinals, a favored nesting spot.

Adaptable from full sun to full shade, drought tolerant once established, and wind resistant - one of the most forgiving natives for a Florida landscape, often used as a hedge in place of non-native boxwood.
Walter's Viburnum is the native counterpart to the park's non-native Sweet Viburnum (PSBP-00119) - same family, but this one belongs here.
origin: Native to Florida and the southeastern coastal plain (South Carolina, Georgia, Alabama, and Florida, except the Keys). Found in pine flatwoods, hydric hammocks, riverbanks, swamp borders, and drier uplands. Member of Viburnaceae, the same family as the park's non-native Sweet Viburnum.
(Older plant labels and field guides may place it in the honeysuckle family, Caprifoliaceae, or in Adoxaceae - it has since been moved to Viburnaceae.)
more_information: Walter's Viburnum is named for Thomas Walter (1740-1789), an English-born planter and botanist in South Carolina who described the species in his Flora Caroliniana.

It is one of the most adaptable native shrubs in Florida - evergreen to semi-evergreen (it drops its old leaves late in winter, just as the new ones push out), growing from seasonally wet flatwoods to dry uplands, in full sun or full shade.

Its early-spring flush of small white flower clusters is showy enough to have earned the nickname Florida snow, and it arrives when little else in the flatwoods is blooming - making it a critical early-season pollen source for native bees emerging before the rest of the springtime buffet opens.

Pollinators work the flowers for pollen more than nectar, since viburnum flowers are shallow and produce little nectar. The flat drupes that follow ripen from red to black through summer and fall and are taken by a range of birds.

Left alone, the plant forms a dense, rounded shrub that can sucker into a thicket and eventually grow into a small tree to about 20 feet, occasionally taller. It tolerates hard pruning, which is why it is widely used as a native hedge or screen - a Florida-friendly stand-in for clipped boxwood. Dwarf cultivars such as 'Densa' are also sold.
wildlife_value: A strong year-round wildlife plant. The spring flowers provide pollen and some nectar for bees and butterflies, and the species is a documented larval host for the spring azure butterfly (Celastrina ladon). The summer-to-fall drupes feed songbirds and other wildlife, and the dense, fine foliage is a favored nesting and cover site for birds, including cardinals.

Spring azure caterpillars run a striking partnership with ants: they secrete a sugary fluid that ants feed on, and in return the ants guard the caterpillars from wasps and spiders - a tiny hired bodyguard service playing out on the foliage.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, white, fragrant, in flat-topped clusters 2 to 3 inches across at the branch tips in late winter to early spring. Showy in mass.

blocks.label: A flower-cluster trick
blocks.text: Look closely at a flower cluster: the showy blooms around the outer edge are often larger but sterile, while the small fertile flowers sit in the center. The plant grows a billboard of bigger fake flowers to pull pollinators in from a distance, then puts them to work on the tiny real ones once they land.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: A flattened drupe ripening from green to red to black through summer and fall. Eaten by birds.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Small, dark green, leathery, ovate to spatulate (wider near the tip), about 1 to 2 inches long, opposite, with entire or slightly toothed margins; tiny glandular dots beneath. Evergreen to semi-evergreen, dropping old leaves late in winter as new growth appears.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: Dense, fine-textured shrub to small tree, often suckering into a thicket; takes hard pruning as a hedge.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: How to spot it in the wild: a dense, small-leaved shrub smothered in small white flower clusters in early spring, followed by red-to-black berries. The fine foliage and thicket habit set it apart from the larger-leaved, non-native Sweet Viburnum.

size.spread: 6 to 10 feet, wider where it suckers
size.habit: dense, fine-textured evergreen to semi-evergreen shrub or small tree; suckers can form a colony
size.height: commonly 8 to 20 feet; can reach about 25 to 30 feet as a small tree, or be kept low as a clipped hedge

growing_conditions.light: full sun to full shade - one of the most light-adaptable natives
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: wide range; moist to seasonally wet sandy, clay, or calcareous soils, and drier uplands; tolerates poor soils
growing_conditions.cold_tolerance: excellent; USDA zones 7 to 10
growing_conditions.note: tolerates hard pruning; suckers can be kept in check with timely pruning.

detail: Not a food plant for humans. The drupes are an important food for birds and other wildlife.

people: No significant toxicity to people documented.
dogs: No significant toxicity to dogs documented.

invasive.notes: Florida native. Not invasive.

internal_notes: PARK CONTEXT: Location to be confirmed. iNat observation exists. Story belongs here once location confirmed — likely the tall row around office/galleria previously attributed to Sweet Viburnum.

butterfly.notes: Masses of small white spring flowers offer nectar to small butterflies and bees; may host spring azure — recheck candidate.

quick_hits: The leathery red fruit is packed with hundreds of juicy seeds, each wrapped in its own jewel-like sac of edible pulp.
It is one of the oldest cultivated fruits on Earth — grown and prized for more than 5,000 years.
The many-seeded fruit has long been a symbol of abundance and fertility across cultures from Persia to the Mediterranean.
The word "grenade" comes from the pomegranate — early grenades were named for the fruit they resembled, seeds and all.
origin: Native to a region stretching from Iran (Persia) through northern India and into Central Asia. Cultivated since antiquity, it spread across the Mediterranean, the Middle East, and eventually the warm Americas. It grows well in Florida's warm climate, though it fruits best where summers are drier.
more_information: The pomegranate is among the most ancient of cultivated fruits, with a history reaching back more than five thousand years across Persia, the Mediterranean, and South Asia. Its many seeds made it a near-universal symbol of abundance, fertility, and renewal, woven into mythology, art, and religious texts.

Each fruit is a tidy piece of engineering. Inside the tough rind, hundreds of seeds are each surrounded by a translucent sac of tart-sweet juice called an aril — the part you actually eat — separated by bitter white membranes.
There is a language footnote hiding in the fruit. The pomegranate's old French name, "pomme grenade" (seeded apple), gave us the word grenade for the seed-filled explosive it was thought to resemble.
wildlife_value: The bright tubular flowers are rich in nectar and draw bees, butterflies, and occasionally hummingbirds. Ripe and split fruit feeds birds and small mammals. The dense, twiggy shrub also offers nesting and shelter cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A deciduous large shrub or small tree, often multi-trunked, with thin branches and sometimes thorny twigs. Expect it to drop its leaves completely in winter; fresh bronze-tinted growth pushes out again in early spring.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Showy, waxy, orange-red, trumpet-shaped blooms in late spring and summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, leathery-skinned, yellow-brown to deep red, crowned with the dried flower calyx; packed with seeds in juicy red arils.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Narrow, glossy, opposite, bronze-tinted when young, turning yellow before dropping. The plant is fully deciduous — a bare winter silhouette is completely normal and gives way to fresh new growth each spring.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A twiggy shrub with vivid orange-red flowers in summer and round, crown-tipped fruit later in the season. The little "crown" on top of the fruit is the giveaway.

size.habit: multi-trunked shrub or small tree, often suckering

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: Fruits best in hot, drier summers. While the plant thrives as a beautiful ornamental shrub in Bradenton, our intense summer humidity and heavy rainfall frequently cause fruit to split, rot, or develop fungal spot before it fully ripens — making this more of a flowering ornamental than a reliable fruiting plant on the West Coast of Florida.

detail: Edible. The juicy arils around each seed are eaten fresh or juiced; the tart rind and membranes are not.

people: No known toxicity to people in normal use. The fruit is a common food.
dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The fruit may cause mild stomach upset if a dog eats a large amount, especially the rind.

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372612057 (david_mcswain); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.
safety_note: Pomegranate is a common food — the juicy arils around each seed are eaten fresh or juiced, while the tart rind and membranes are not.
It isn't listed as toxic to dogs, though a dog that eats a large amount, especially the rind, may get a mild stomach upset.

quick_hits: Slice the fruit crosswise and every piece comes out a perfect five-pointed star.
Anyone with kidney disease or reduced kidney function — including undiagnosed cases — should never eat starfruit; even a single fruit can trigger seizures or worse.

Starfruit carries caramboxin, a neurotoxin that healthy kidneys filter out, but large quantities consumed on an empty stomach have caused acute kidney injury even in people with normal renal function.
The tree fruits heavily, sometimes producing hundreds of pounds of star-shaped fruit in a single year.
origin: Native to tropical Southeast Asia, likely originating in the Malay Peninsula or Indonesia. It has been cultivated across the tropics for centuries and grows well in warm, frost-free parts of Florida.
more_information: Carambola, better known as starfruit, is grown as much for its looks as its flavor — sliced crosswise, it yields flawless edible stars that turn an ordinary fruit salad into a centerpiece. The fruit is crisp, juicy, and ranges from tart to sweet depending on variety and ripeness.

Behind the cheerful shape sits a genuine medical caution. Starfruit contains caramboxin, a neurotoxin, and oxalic acid. The risk is greatest for anyone with kidney disease or reduced renal function — including people who may not yet know their kidneys are impaired — for whom even a small amount can cause hiccups, confusion, seizures, and in serious cases death.

Medical literature also documents acute kidney injury in previously healthy individuals who consumed very large quantities, particularly on an empty stomach. For most people a serving is a harmless treat, but the warning is worth knowing before eating fruit that may have fallen on a public trail.
The tree is a generous producer, flowering and fruiting through much of the warm season and often carrying flowers and fruit at the same time.
wildlife_value: The small lilac flowers attract bees and other pollinators, and ripe or fallen fruit feeds birds and small mammals. As a dense evergreen, it offers good cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A small, bushy evergreen tree with a rounded, often drooping canopy.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, lilac to pink, in clusters on branches and twigs, sometimes straight from the trunk.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Waxy, yellow-green to golden, with five (sometimes six) prominent ribs; star-shaped in cross-section.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnately compound with soft oval leaflets that fold down at night.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A small tree hung with ribbed, waxy yellow fruit. Slice one across to reveal the star — the unmistakable signature of carambola.

size.habit: rounded, bushy, often drooping

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive — protect from hard freezes

detail: Edible and popular eaten fresh, juiced, or used as a garnish. See the toxicity section for an important safety note before consuming, especially for anyone with kidney concerns.

people: Starfruit contains caramboxin (a neurotoxin) and oxalates. Anyone with kidney disease or impaired renal function — including undiagnosed cases — should avoid it entirely; even small amounts can cause hiccups, confusion, seizures, and potentially fatal reactions.

Even in people with normal kidney function, consuming very large quantities, especially on an empty stomach, has caused acute kidney injury in documented clinical cases. For most healthy adults eating a normal serving, the risk is low — but this fruit warrants real awareness.
dogs: Use caution with pets. The fruit's soluble oxalates bind with calcium in a dog's body and can cause sudden kidney failure, especially if dogs forage on fallen fruit. Keep fallen fruit picked up if dogs visit the area.

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372614267 (harrisonmello); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.
safety_note: Important caution: starfruit contains caramboxin, a neurotoxin, along with oxalates. Anyone with kidney disease or reduced kidney function — including undiagnosed cases — should avoid it entirely.
In those cases even a small amount can cause hiccups, confusion, seizures, and potentially fatal reactions.
Even with normal kidneys, eating very large quantities on an empty stomach has caused acute kidney injury.
For most healthy adults a normal serving is low-risk, but the fruit deserves real awareness.
For dogs it's a genuine hazard: the soluble oxalates can trigger sudden kidney failure, especially from foraging fallen fruit. Keep fallen fruit picked up where dogs visit.

quick_hits: In late spring it erupts into long, hanging chains of brilliant golden-yellow flowers, like a tree dripping in sunshine.
It is the national tree and flower of Thailand, where its blooms symbolize royalty and good fortune.
The long, dark seed pods that follow can reach two feet — each one divided inside into dozens of individual sealed cells, one seed per chamber, all suspended in thick, sticky pulp.
The sticky pulp inside those pods is a traditional, and very effective, natural laxative.
origin: Native to South and Southeast Asia, from India and Sri Lanka through Myanmar and Thailand. Long cultivated as an ornamental across the tropics for its spectacular bloom, and grown in frost-free parts of Florida.
more_information: The golden shower tree earns its name once a year, when it covers itself in cascading clusters of bright yellow flowers so dense they can nearly hide the foliage. The display is brief but unforgettable, and has made the tree a celebrated ornamental and the national symbol of Thailand.

After flowering come the pods — long, narrow, cylindrical, and nearly black when ripe, sometimes approaching two feet in length, with seeds set in dark, sticky pulp between papery partitions. That pulp (cassia pulp) has a long history in traditional medicine as a gentle but reliable laxative.
It belongs to the pea family, and the flush-then-pod rhythm is a legume signature, even on a tree this showy.
wildlife_value: The abundant nectar-rich flowers draw bees, especially large carpenter bees, and butterflies. As a legume it associates with nitrogen-fixing soil bacteria that enrich the ground around it.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium deciduous to semi-evergreen tree with a spreading, open crown.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Long, pendulous chains (racemes) of fragrant five-petaled golden-yellow flowers in late spring and summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Long, cylindrical, dark brown to black seed pods up to about 2 feet, with seeds in sticky pulp.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnately compound with several pairs of large oval leaflets.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: In bloom, the hanging golden flower chains are unmistakable. Out of bloom, look for the long, dark, sausage-like pods dangling from the branches.

size.habit: spreading, open, rounded crown

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive when young

detail: Not a food plant. The pod pulp is used medicinally as a laxative, not eaten casually.

people: Mild toxicity. The pulp and seeds are a strong laxative — eating much causes cramping, nausea, and diarrhea. Not dangerous in passing, but not for snacking.
dogs: Use caution. The pods and seeds can cause vomiting and diarrhea in dogs (Cassia/Senna group). Keep fallen pods picked up.

invasive.notes: Not considered invasive in Florida.

internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372720634 (flowerchild1969); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

butterfly.notes: A Cassia/Senna relative — larval host for sulphur butterflies.

safety_note: The pods and seeds act as a strong laxative — eating much causes cramping, nausea, and diarrhea. It isn't dangerous in passing, but it isn't for snacking.
For dogs, the pods and seeds can cause vomiting and diarrhea, like others in the Cassia/Senna group. Keep fallen pods picked up.

quick_hits: Crack the thin tan shell and inside is translucent, juicy flesh wrapped around a single glossy black seed — peeled, it looks startlingly like a real pupil and iris, which is exactly what the Cantonese name lóngyǎn means: dragon's eye.

Longans are notoriously erratic bloomers, but growers found a wild fix: a soil drench of potassium chlorate — the same compound used in matches and fireworks — reliably shocks the tree into bloom on demand, even off-season.
It is the close cousin of the lychee, with a similar sweet, musky flavor.
The fruit hangs in heavy drooping clusters, sometimes by the hundreds.
species.origin: Native to southern Asia, from southern China through Southeast Asia. Long cultivated for its fruit and now grown commercially in tropical and subtropical regions, including South and Central Florida.
more_information: Longan is a member of the soapberry family and a close relative of the lychee and rambutan. Its fruit is smaller and less flashy than lychee but keeps and ships better, and many find the flavor sweeter and muskier.

The name "dragon's eye" comes from the peeled fruit: a sphere of translucent, pale flesh with a single round, glossy black seed showing through the center — convincingly eye-like. The flesh is eaten fresh, dried, or in soups and desserts across Asia.
The tree is a heavy, sometimes erratic bearer — loading branches with hanging clusters in good years — and prefers a warm climate with a brief cool spell to trigger flowering.
wildlife_value: The small cream flowers are excellent bee plants and a notable nectar and honey source. Ripe and fallen fruit feeds birds and small mammals.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium evergreen tree with a dense, rounded, spreading canopy.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, yellowish-cream, in large branched clusters (panicles); rich in nectar. Each panicle opens in three sequential waves: staminate (male) flowers first, then hermaphroditic flowers acting as females, then a second wave of hermaphroditic flowers acting as males — a relay designed to maximize cross-pollination.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, tan to light brown, thin-shelled, in drooping clusters; pale juicy flesh around one shiny black seed.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnately compound, glossy, with leathery oblong leaflets.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Heavy hanging clusters of round, tan-shelled fruit. Peel one to find the translucent flesh and single dark "dragon's eye" seed.

size.habit: dense, rounded, spreading

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: needs warmth; a brief cool, dry spell helps trigger flowering

edibility.detail: Edible. The translucent flesh is eaten fresh or dried; the single seed is not eaten.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people. A common edible fruit; the hard seed is a choking hazard, not a poison.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The flesh is harmless, but the large round seed is a choking and obstruction hazard — keep fallen fruit picked up.

other_notes: Longan is self-fertile — a single tree will set fruit. However, cross-pollination between trees typically improves crop size and quality.

Nearly all Florida trees are the 'Kohala' cultivar, developed in Hawaii and evaluated for south Florida conditions by UF/IFAS. Several newer Thai cultivars are under evaluation but not yet widely planted.
species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372612360 (david_mcswain); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.
safety_note: The seed is the one thing to watch: longan seeds contain saponins that can cause vomiting, diarrhea, and stomach upset in dogs — and are not edible for people either, being hard and bitter. Keep whole fruit away from dogs; the flesh is fine for people.
The fruit flesh is safe and widely eaten fresh, dried, or canned. The hard shell poses a choking hazard for small animals. Otherwise the tree is harmless to handle.

quick_hits: Ripe, the fruit looks like a green tomato but scoops out like rich chocolate pudding — earning it the name "chocolate pudding fruit."
There is no actual chocolate involved; the flavor and custard texture are entirely the fruit's own.
It must be eaten dead-ripe and soft. Unripe, it is hard, bitter, and astringent.
It is a persimmon relative, and that mahogany-dark, sweet flesh is the family's strangest member.
origin: Native to Mexico, Central America, and Colombia. Long cultivated in its homeland and now grown in tropical and subtropical regions, including South and Central Florida.
more_information: Black sapote is a true persimmon and a member of the ebony family (Ebenaceae) — the same lineage that gives us dense, dark timber prized for centuries. While most persimmons turn bright orange or yellow as they ripen, this species behaves completely differently: its glossy green skin turns dull and muddy as the interior transforms into a dark, sweet, custardy pulp.

Timing is everything. Picked or eaten too soon, the fruit is rock-hard, chalky, and mouth-puckeringly astringent. Allowed to soften until the skin browns and the flesh turns almost black and jelly-like, it becomes a dessert in its own right, often blended with a little citrus or folded into baked goods.
The tree is handsome and glossy-leaved, and its dark, dense wood links it to true ebony, the prized timber of the same genus.
wildlife_value: The small white flowers attract bees and other pollinators. Ripe and dropped fruit is taken by birds and small mammals, and the dense evergreen canopy offers cover.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium to large evergreen tree with a dense, rounded canopy of glossy leaves.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, white to greenish, tucked among the leaves.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Round, tomato-like, olive-green ripening to brown-black; soft dark pudding-like pulp inside.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, leathery, elliptic, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Round green "tomatoes" hanging among glossy leaves. A ripe one is soft and dull-skinned; cut it to find the dark, custardy flesh.

size.habit: dense, rounded

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; prefers warm, frost-free sites

detail: Deliciously edible only when fully ripe, soft, and slightly wrinkled. The flesh can be scooped straight out of the skin or blended into desserts. Unripe fruit is rock-hard and intensely, unpleasantly astringent.

people: No known toxicity to humans. It is not listed as toxic to dogs by the ASPCA; while the ripe flesh is harmless in small amounts, ingestion of the large seeds or unripe fruit can cause mild gastrointestinal distress.
dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by the ASPCA. Ripe flesh is harmless in small amounts; ingestion of the large seeds or unripe fruit can cause mild gastrointestinal distress.

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372611941 (david_mcswain); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.
safety_note: Black sapote is delicious, but only when fully ripe, soft, and slightly wrinkled — the flesh is scooped straight from the skin or blended into desserts.
Unripe fruit is rock-hard and intensely astringent.
It has no known toxicity to people and isn't ASPCA-listed for dogs; the ripe flesh is harmless in small amounts, though the large seeds or unripe fruit can cause mild stomach upset.

quick_hits: A true Florida-native tree that grows with its feet in the water — in swamps, pond edges, and even brackish mangrove margins.
Its yellow-green fruit gives it the names pond apple and alligator apple, after the gators that share its swampy haunts and eat the fallen fruit.
The fruit floats, and so do the seeds — the tree spreads by letting moving water carry its crop downstream.
It is a close cousin of the custard apple and soursop, with similar pale, custard-textured flesh.
origin: Native to Florida, the Caribbean, tropical Central and South America, and West Africa. In Florida it is a characteristic tree of freshwater swamps, pond and lake margins, sloughs, and the upstream edges of mangrove wetlands.
more_information: Pond apple is one of the few trees genuinely at home in standing water. It rings Florida's swamps, ponds, and sloughs and pushes right up to the brackish edge of mangrove country, tolerating flooding and some salt where most trees would drown. Its trunk often flares into a swollen, buttressed base for stability in soft, wet ground.

The common names tell the story of its place. "Pond apple" marks its habitat; "alligator apple" nods to the gators that share the swamp and eat the dropped fruit. The yellow-green fruit is apple- to melon-sized, with soft, pale flesh.
Water does the dispersing. Both the buoyant fruit and its flattened seeds float, drifting on swamp currents and tides to sprout elsewhere — a neat fit for a tree that lives where the water moves.

Before early-20th-century drainage transformed South Florida, a dense belt of pond apple forest — locals called it the Custard Apple country — stretched roughly 32,000 acres along the southern and southeastern shore of Lake Okeechobee. That forest was cleared for what became the Everglades Agricultural Area, and nearly all of it is gone.
The largest remaining concentration now grows in Everglades National Park.

Pond apple's toughness has made it useful beyond its native swamps. Because of its hardiness and tolerance of wet, brackish soils, it is used as a grafting rootstock for less-hardy Annona relatives such as soursop and sugar apple, lending them its disease resistance and flood tolerance.
wildlife_value: A valuable native wetland tree. Its fruit is eaten by raccoons, birds, turtles, fish, and alligators, and it is the larval host for the giant sphinx moth (Cocytius antaeus), the largest sphinx moth in North America. Growing in flooded ground, it provides cover, perches, and nesting habitat at the water's edge and helps stabilize swampy shorelines.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A small to medium deciduous to semi-evergreen wetland tree (bare-branched in winter), often with a swollen, buttressed base, growing in or beside water.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Solitary, pale yellow to cream, three-petaled, with a reddish inner blotch.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Yellow-green, apple- to melon-sized, with soft pale custard-like flesh; floats.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, leathery, oblong, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A glossy-leaved tree standing in or right beside the water, often with a flared base, hung with yellow-green apple-like fruit. Its waterside footing is itself the best clue. In winter, when the tree drops its leaves, look for the distinctly swollen, buttressed trunk base standing right out of the water.

size.habit: upright, often with a buttressed base

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: muck · sand · tolerates prolonged flooding and some brackish water
growing_conditions.drought_tolerance: practically none — thrives in standing water where most trees drown; flooding tolerance is high
growing_conditions.note: a wetland specialist for pond margins and swampy ground

detail: he ripe fruit pulp is technically edible but mealy and bland—far more appealing to wildlife than to people. However, the seeds, bark, and leaves contain toxic compounds (annonaceous acetogenins) and must never be consumed. Use caution and keep pets from chewing on fallen fruit or seeds; while not specifically listed by the ASPCA, the seed toxicity warrants the same caution as for people.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #368070146 (denise_sosa); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.
species.native_notes: Confirmed native to Florida by FNPS, the Atlas of Florida Plants (USF), and multiple botanical sources; naturally occurs in south Florida swamps and wetlands.
species.watch_invasive_notes: Annona glabra is a Florida native and does not appear on the FISC (formerly FLEPPC) Category I or II exotic pest plant list; it is invasive in Australia and parts of Asia but not listed as invasive or a plant-to-watch in Florida.

butterfly.notes: Annona glabra is recorded as a larval host for Tailed Jay (Graphium agamemnon) and Common Jay (Graphium doson) in South and Southeast Asia, but those butterfly species do not occur in Florida; no North American or Florida-range butterfly species have been documented using Annona glabra as a larval host or notable nectar source at the latitude of Bradenton.

safety_note: The ripe pulp is technically edible but mealy and bland — far more appealing to wildlife than to people.
The seeds, bark, and leaves are the hazard: they contain toxic acetogenins and must never be eaten.
Keep pets from chewing fallen fruit or seeds. It isn't specifically ASPCA-listed, but the seed toxicity warrants the same caution as for people.

quick_hits: The avocado is a berry — botanically a single-seeded berry — despite its size and buttery, savory flesh.
Its flowers play a clever trick: each opens as female one day and male the next, timing the switch to avoid pollinating itself.
origin: Native to south-central Mexico and Central America, where it has been cultivated for thousands of years. Now grown worldwide in warm climates, with Florida (especially the south) a major U.S. growing region.
more_information: The avocado has been part of Mesoamerican life for millennia, long enough that its evolutionary partners — the giant ground sloths and other megafauna that once swallowed the fruit whole and dispersed its huge seed — have been extinct for thousands of years. The tree carries on, now spread by people instead.

Its flowers are a marvel of anti-inbreeding design. Each flower opens twice: functioning as female on the first opening and male on the second, with the timing staggered so a tree tends to shed pollen when its own flowers are receptive as female, encouraging cross-pollination between trees.

Unusually for a fruit, the avocado stores its energy as oil rather than sugar, which gives the flesh its rich, buttery quality. One caution worth posting: the leaves, bark, skin, and pit contain a compound called persin that is harmless to people in the fruit but toxic to many animals, birds most of all.
wildlife_value: The small flowers are visited by bees and other insects. Birds and mammals eat fallen fruit, and the large evergreen canopy provides shade, cover, and nesting sites.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A medium to large evergreen tree with a broad, often spreading or dense canopy.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Small, greenish-yellow, in large branched clusters; each flower opens as female then male.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: A large, pear-shaped to round berry with leathery green to black skin, buttery flesh, and one very large seed.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, glossy, elliptic, dark green.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A leafy evergreen tree hung with pear-shaped fruit and a single oversized pit inside. Cut fruit shows the smooth pale-green to yellow flesh.

size.habit: spreading to upright, dense

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · slightly alkaline to acidic · must be well-drained
growing_conditions.note: hates wet feet — root rot is the main hazard in Florida; frost-sensitive

detail: Edible. The buttery flesh is widely eaten; the skin and pit are not eaten.

people: No toxicity to people from the fruit. (The leaves and pit contain persin, but people eat only the flesh.)
dogs: Use caution. Avocado leaves, skin, and pit contain persin, which can cause vomiting and diarrhea in dogs, and the large pit is a serious choking and obstruction hazard. Avocado is far more dangerous to birds (ASPCA).

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

other_notes: The avocado belongs to the laurel family (Lauraceae), which puts it in close botanical company with several other park specimens including sassafras, spicebush, and redbay. That shared family membership also means it faces laurel wilt, the same fungal disease currently threatening native redbay populations across Florida and the Southeast.
internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #368070571 (denise_sosa); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

butterfly.notes: Persea americana is in the family Lauraceae, which hosts some Papilionid larvae in Asia (e.g., Graphium sarpedon) and specialist Phyllocnistis leaf-mining micro-moths in the Neotropics, but no North American or Florida butterfly or moth species is documented using cultivated avocado as a larval host at Bradenton latitudes; avocado flowers attract generalist bees but are not noted as a significant butterfly nectar source.

safety_note: The leaves, bark, skin, and seed of the avocado contain a natural compound called persin. While entirely safe and highly nutritious for people, persin is seriously toxic to birds, rabbits, and livestock, often causing fatal heart damage. Keep pet birds far away from any part of this tree.
Dogs and cats are significantly less sensitive to persin, but the large pit poses a severe choking and intestinal obstruction hazard, and consuming leaves or bark can cause digestive upset.

quick_hits: A pineapple is not one fruit but many — dozens of separate flowers fused together into a single juicy mass.
It is a bromeliad, a cousin of Spanish moss and the air plants that perch in Florida's oak trees.
It takes its time: a plant needs about two years to produce one fruit.
The enzyme bromelain in fresh pineapple actually digests protein — which is why fresh pineapple can make your tongue tingle.
species.origin: Native to South America, likely the Paraná-Paraguay River region of Brazil and Paraguay. Carried across the tropics by early peoples and now grown worldwide; it thrives in warm, frost-free Florida.
more_information: Before it was a grocery-store staple, the pineapple was one of Europe's most coveted luxury objects. After Columbus carried the fruit to Spain, a single pineapple in 17th- and 18th-century Britain could cost the equivalent of thousands of dollars today — far too precious to slice open.

Hosts placed them as centerpieces on dining tables simply to be admired, and a rental market sprang up so that those outside the aristocracy could hire a pineapple by the evening to display their status.

There is a Florida twist hiding in the family tree. Pineapple's wild cousins — the native airplants and Spanish moss draped through Florida's oaks — are under siege from the "evil weevil" (the Mexican bromeliad weevil), an invasive beetle that arrived on imported bromeliads in 1989 and has killed off native bromeliads across the state; several Florida airplants are now listed as endangered.
The weevil will even bore into a pineapple.

The crown of leaves topping every fruit is not just decorative — it is a ready-made cutting. Twist it free, let the base dry for a day or two, then root it in water or plant it directly in fast-draining soil. In frost-free Florida it can go straight into the ground; elsewhere it makes a striking container plant, though fruiting still takes the better part of two years even under ideal conditions.
wildlife_value: The tubular flowers can attract hummingbirds and bees in bloom. The spiny rosette offers some ground-level cover, and ripe fruit draws insects and small animals.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A low, ground-dwelling rosette of stiff, sword-shaped leaves, often spiny-edged.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Many small purple-blue flowers on a central spike, fusing into the fruit.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: The familiar multiple fruit — fused, juicy, golden inside — topped with a leafy crown.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Long, narrow, rigid, gray-green, in a spreading rosette.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Vegetative (months 1–14): a dense, arching ground rosette of rigid sword-shaped leaves with sharp, saw-toothed margins — no stalk yet. In bloom (around months 15–18): look deep into the center for a stout stalk rising up, bearing a cone-like cluster of small purple-blue flowers opening a few at a time from the bottom up. Fruiting (months 18–24): the classic green-to-golden multiple fruit swells on that central stalk, crowned with its own miniature tuft of leaves.

size.habit: clumping ground rosette

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · well-drained; tolerates poor soil
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; the leafy crown can be planted to grow a new plant

edibility.detail: Edible. The juicy golden fruit is eaten fresh, cooked, or juiced.

toxicity.people: No toxicity to people. Fresh fruit contains bromelain, which can irritate the mouth in excess but is not dangerous.
toxicity.dogs: Not toxic to dogs (ASPCA lists pineapple as non-toxic). The tough core and spiny leaves can cause stomach upset or injury if chewed.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #368068031 (mariaaaz); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

butterfly.notes: Two Castniidae moths — Giant Pineapple Borer (Castniomera licus) and Pineapple Moth Borer (Castnia icarus) — are documented stem-boring larval pests of Ananas comosus in South America, but neither occurs in Florida or at Bradenton's latitude. No North American or Florida butterfly species is recorded as using pineapple as a larval host. Pineapple flowers do produce accessible nectar, but no sources document the plant as a notable or frequently visited adult butterfly nectar source in Florida gardens.

quick_hits: Jackfruit produces the largest fruit of any tree on Earth — a single one can weigh over 100 pounds.
Those giant fruits grow straight from the trunk and main branches, not out on the twigs — a trick called cauliflory that lets the wood bear the weight.

Ripe jackfruit floods the air with isoamyl acetate, the same fruity compound that gives Juicy Fruit gum its signature smell — a coincidence so uncanny that fans have debated the connection for decades.

That enormous jackfruit is not really one fruit at all. It is a fused cluster of hundreds of individual flowers, each pollinated flower swelling its perianth into one of those sweet golden bulbs you eat.
species.origin: Native to the rainforests of the Western Ghats of India and surrounding South and Southeast Asia. Cultivated across the tropics for thousands of years and grown in frost-free South and Central Florida.
more_information: The jackfruit is a record-holder: the largest tree-borne fruit in the world, with individual fruits regularly topping fifty pounds and sometimes exceeding a hundred. To carry that weight, the tree does something unusual — it produces fruit directly on the trunk and major limbs (a habit called cauliflory) rather than on slender outer branches.

Inside the knobby green rind are dozens of sweet, golden, edible bulbs surrounding large seeds, all embedded in stringy fibers. Ripe jackfruit is fragrant and sweet; young, unripe jackfruit is starchy and neutral, and when cooked it pulls apart into tender shreds that have made it a popular plant-based stand-in for pulled meat.

What looks like a single fruit is botanically a syncarp — a multiple fruit formed from an entire inflorescence of tightly packed flowers. Each sweet, golden bulb is the swollen perianth of one successfully pollinated female flower; the stringy rag surrounding them represents flowers that did not develop. A single jackfruit can contain the fused remains of thousands of individual blooms.

Pollination here is a three-way mutualism. A tiny gall midge, Clinodiplosis ultracrepidata, is attracted to both male and female flower spikes by similar scent compounds. On the male spikes, a parasitic fungus (Rhizopus artocarpi) takes hold, and the midges feed on it and lay their eggs there.

Female spikes offer no fungus and no reward — they simply mimic the male scent closely enough to lure the pollen-dusted midges in anyway. The whole elaborate system was only formally described in 2018.
Every part of the tree exudes a sticky white latex, so harvesting and cutting jackfruit is traditionally done with oiled hands and knives to keep the glue at bay.
wildlife_value: Fallen and overripe fruit is a magnet for insects, birds, and mammals. The large evergreen canopy provides dense shade, cover, and nesting habitat.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A large evergreen tree with a dense, rounded crown.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Tiny, in dense fleshy spikes borne on the trunk and branches; male and female spikes on the same tree. A gall midge (Clinodiplosis ultracrepidata) pollinates both, lured by matching scents — though only male spikes offer a fungal food reward.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Enormous, oblong, green to yellow-brown, knobby-skinned syncarp growing from the trunk and limbs. It is not a single fruit but hundreds of individual flowers fused together — each sweet golden bulb is the swollen perianth of one pollinated flower.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large, glossy, leathery, dark green, oval.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Huge knobby fruit attached directly to the trunk and big branches — a sight no other park tree offers. Look closer for the smaller, club-like male and female flower spikes growing side by side on the same limbs. Glossy dark leaves and milky sap confirm it.

size.habit: dense, rounded, spreading

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · acidic to slightly alkaline · deep, well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; needs room — the fruit is heavy and the tree is large

edibility.detail: Edible. Ripe bulbs are eaten fresh; unripe fruit is cooked as a vegetable and meat substitute; the seeds are eaten boiled or roasted.

toxicity.people: No known toxicity to people. The sticky latex is messy and can irritate skin but is not poisonous.
toxicity.dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. The flesh is harmless in small amounts; large seeds are a choking hazard and the latex can upset a dog's stomach.

species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #370306609 (randall_carter); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

larval_species: Io Moth relative / Saturniid Moth (Automeris metzli)
butterfly.notes: Documented larval hosts are moths (Lepidoptera), not true butterflies: Glyphodes caesalis (Crambidae) is the well-known Jackfruit Borer recorded across tropical Asia on Artocarpus heterophyllus, and Automeris metzli (Saturniidae) was reported in 2025 as a new stinging-caterpillar pest on jackfruit in Panama. Neither species is established or expected at Bradenton latitudes. No true butterfly (Papilionoidea) has been documented using jackfruit as a larval host in any reviewed source. Adult nectar use by butterflies is unsupported: jackfruit flowers are inconspicuous and pollinated primarily by gall midges (Clinodiplosis ultracrepidata), not by Lepidoptera.

quick_hits: This is the Florida state tree — the cabbage palm, found in every county in the state.
The growing bud at its center is the original "swamp cabbage," or heart of palm, a regional delicacy.
Harvesting that heart, though, kills the palm, since it is the single growing point the whole tree depends on.
Its criss-crossed leaf bases ("boots") create perches and pockets that ferns, vines, and small animals colonize.
origin: Native to the southeastern United States, throughout Florida and along the coastal plain. It grows in an exceptionally wide range of habitats — hammocks, marsh edges, dunes, and pine flatwoods — and tolerates salt, flooding, drought, and fire.
more_information: The cabbage palm is Florida's state tree and arguably its most characteristic, growing from the Keys to the Panhandle and shrugging off salt spray, flooding, drought, hurricanes, and fire. That toughness comes from a single, protected growing point (the "heart" or terminal bud) buried at the top of the trunk.

That heart is also the catch. Cut from the tree, the tender bud is the famous "swamp cabbage" or heart of palm — a Cracker Florida tradition still celebrated at festivals. But because a palm has only that one growing point, harvesting the heart kills the whole tree, which is why it is a treat to be taken thoughtfully and never from wild stands without care.

Older trunks may keep their crisscrossing old leaf bases, called boots, or shed them to a smooth gray column. Either way the palm is a habitat in itself — boots and leaf crowns host ferns, vines, insects, and nesting birds.
Two more notes worth knowing: it is one of the most hurricane-resistant trees in Florida, and it is the official state tree of two states — Florida and South Carolina, the "Palmetto State."
wildlife_value: The big sprays of white flowers are a major nectar source for bees (the basis of prized palm honey) and many insects. The black fruits feed raccoons, birds, and other wildlife, and the leaf-base "boots" and crown shelter ferns, insects, bats, and nesting birds.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A single-trunked fan palm with a rounded crown of large, arching leaves.

blocks.label: Trunk
blocks.text: Stout, gray, either smooth or covered in crisscrossing old leaf bases ("boots").

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Long, branching sprays of small, fragrant, creamy-white flowers in summer.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Small, round, shiny black drupes in large hanging clusters.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Large costapalmate (fan-like with an arching midrib), giving the leaf a folded, curved look.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: A classic fan palm with a single gray trunk and curved, fan-shaped leaves. The arching midrib that runs partway into the fan separates it from true fan palms.

size.habit: single-trunked, rounded crown

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · clay · loam · acidic to alkaline · tolerates flooding and drought
growing_conditions.note: extremely tough — handles salt, flood, drought, and fire

detail: The growing bud (swamp cabbage / heart of palm) is edible and a Florida tradition — but harvesting it kills the palm. The small fruits are also edible but bland.

people: No toxicity to people.
dogs: Not listed as toxic to dogs by ASPCA. Cabbage palm is not known to be harmful.

invasive.notes: Native to Florida — the state tree. Not invasive.

internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #370339474 (ruby_meador); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

butterfly.notes: Florida's state tree and a confirmed larval host for the Monk Skipper, whose caterpillars feed on the palm foliage; the monk skipper ranges throughout peninsular Florida including Bradenton's latitude. The large fragrant flower panicles attract bees, flies, wasps, and beetles as the primary pollinators, and general sources indicate insects including butterflies visit the flowers for nectar, but no specific adult butterfly species are documented as consistent nectar visitors in authoritative Florida sources. The cabbage palm caterpillar (Litoprosopus futilis, an owlet moth, not a butterfly) also uses Sabal palmetto inflorescences as a larval host and is noted separately. Retained leaf bases (boots) shelter ferns and wildlife.

safety_note: The growing bud — swamp cabbage, or heart of palm — is edible and a Florida tradition, but harvesting it kills the palm. The small fruits are edible but bland.
It has no known toxicity to people or dogs.

alternate_names: Star Jasmine (a name shared with other plants)
quick_hits: Its flowers are 2–3 times larger than those of most other jasmines, with up to 11 slender, narrow corolla lobes radiating from a reddish-purple bud — a scale that surprises visitors expecting a small, modest bloom.
The scent is sweetest in the evening, when the blooms perfume the air around them.
A scrambling, semi-vining shrub, it can be grown as a mound, a hedge, or a loose climber.
Despite sharing a common name with several unrelated plants, this one is the real thing — a genuine member of the genus Jasminum.
origin: Native to South and Southeast Asia. Widely cultivated across the tropics and subtropics as a fragrant ornamental, and grown in warm, frost-free Florida.
more_information: Angel-wing jasmine is prized for its long blooming season and intense fragrance. A genuine member of the genus Jasminum, it is easily distinguished from lookalikes like Confederate jasmine (Trachelospermum jasminoides) by its significantly larger flowers with longer, more slender petals and its glossier, simple (non-pinnate) leaves.

Left unpruned, its flexible woody branches will scramble along the ground as a dense cover, but it can just as easily be trained up a trellis or sheared into a tidy, mounding hedge. The name Jasminum nitidum — still widely used in Florida nurseries and landscapes — refers to the same plant, making both names worth knowing.
wildlife_value: The fragrant, night-scented flowers attract moths, butterflies, and other pollinators. The dense, scrambling growth provides cover and nesting sites for small birds.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A scrambling, semi-vining evergreen shrub.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: White, pinwheel-shaped, fragrant; up to 11 slender corolla lobes extend from a central tube, opening from reddish-purple buds in clusters. The underside of the lobes often retains a soft purplish or pinkish tint even when fully open. Most fragrant in the evening.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Glossy, dark green, oval, in opposite or whorled arrangement.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: White pinwheel flowers paired with dark purple-red buds on a glossy, loosely climbing shrub. Look closely at the underside of the open corolla lobes — a soft purplish or pinkish flush lingers there, a reliable field mark for this species. The evening fragrance confirms a true jasmine.

size.habit: scrambling, semi-vining, mounding

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive; can be grown as a hedge, mound, or light climber

detail: Not an edible plant. Grown strictly as a fragrant ornamental.

people: No known toxicity to humans. Unlike other plants sharing the 'jasmine' common name — such as the highly toxic Carolina jessamine (Gelsemium sempervirens) — true jasmines (Jasminum) are safe for residential spaces.
dogs: Listed as non-toxic to dogs and cats by the ASPCA.
toxicity.cats: Listed as non-toxic to cats and dogs by the ASPCA.

invasive.notes: Not considered invasive in Florida.

internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #370309291 (randall_carter); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.

butterfly.notes: Fragrant night-scented flowers; not a notable butterfly plant.

safety_note: This is a fragrant ornamental, not a food plant, so it isn't eaten — but it's genuinely harmless.
True jasmines (Jasminum) have no known toxicity to people, unlike unrelated plants that share the 'jasmine' name, such as the highly toxic Carolina jessamine.
The ASPCA lists it as non-toxic to dogs and cats as well. Handle it freely.

quick_hits: It looks like a stiff, low palm or fern, but it is a cycad — an ancient seed plant from the age of the dinosaurs.
The thick, leathery leaflets feel almost like cardboard, which is where the name comes from.
Every part is highly poisonous to people and pets — the seeds especially can be deadly to dogs.
Like all cycads, it partners with bacteria in special roots to pull nitrogen from the air, letting it thrive in poor soil.
origin: Native to a small area of eastern Mexico (Veracruz). Despite its limited wild range, it is one of the most widely planted ornamental cycads in warm climates, including Florida.
more_information: Cycads are living fossils — a lineage of seed plants that flourished alongside the dinosaurs and have changed little since. The cardboard cycad is one of the most popular in cultivation, prized for its low, symmetrical rosette of stiff, dark green, pinnate leaves whose thick, blunt leaflets have a distinctly papery, cardboard-like feel.

Beneath the handsome looks is a serious hazard. Every part of the plant contains cycasin and related toxins, and it is dangerously poisonous to people, dogs, cats, and livestock. The seeds are the most toxic part and are a frequent cause of severe, often fatal poisoning in dogs that chew them.

Cardboard cycad is pollinated not by wind but by a specialized snout weevil, Rhopalotria mollis, apparently introduced from Mexico alongside its host. The weevils complete their life cycle inside the aromatic male cones, feeding on pollen and cone tissue, and carry pollen to female cones as they move between plants — a tight, obligate partnership between plant and insect.

The cardboard cycad's genus, Zamia, includes the only cycad native to the United States — the coontie (Zamia integrifolia), a Florida native and the primary host plant of the atala butterfly (Eumaeus atala).

Atala larvae feed on coontie leaves and sequester the plant's cycasin toxins in their own bodies, making both caterpillar and adult butterfly unpalatable to birds — a chemical defense borrowed directly from the plant.
Where coontie is scarce, atala butterflies have been documented using other ornamental cycads, including the cardboard cycad, as supplemental larval hosts.

So while coontie is the cornerstone of the atala's recovery — it was nearly wiped out by over-harvesting for Florida arrowroot starch, almost taking the butterfly with it — a cardboard cycad growing nearby can offer additional footing for one of Florida's great conservation comeback stories.
wildlife_value: Wildlife value is limited by the plant's toxicity. In its native range, specialized cycad-feeding insects and beetles pollinate the cones; the toxic seeds are not a safe food source for local wildlife and pets.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A low, stiff, palm-like cycad with a short, often partly buried trunk and a symmetrical rosette of leaves.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Pinnate, dark green, with thick, leathery, blunt, cardboard-textured leaflets; new leaves emerge in a flush.

blocks.label: Reproduction
blocks.text: By cones, not flowers; male and female cones on separate plants.

blocks.label: Seeds
blocks.text: Borne in the female cone; large and highly toxic.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Start with the leaflets: pinch one gently — it should feel unusually rigid, thick, and blunt, more like heavy construction paper than a typical leaf. Next, look at the center of the rosette for new growth: emerging leaf flushes coil tightly and are dusted in a dense, rust-colored fuzz (the 'mealy' texture behind the species name furfuracea). On mature plants, check the center for cones rather than flowers — male cones are narrower and cluster upright like fingers, while female cones are stout and barrel-shaped and, when ripe, yield the distinctive bright orange-red seeds. The cardboard-feel leaflet plus the cone (never a flower) is the definitive tell.

size.habit: low, spreading rosette

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · well-drained; tolerates poor soil
growing_conditions.note: frost-sensitive in hard freezes; very low-maintenance once established

detail: Not edible — highly toxic. No part of the plant should ever be eaten.

people: Highly toxic if ingested. Contains cycasin and related toxins that can cause severe vomiting, liver damage, and neurological effects. The seeds are the most dangerous part. Seek immediate medical care if any part is eaten.
dogs: Highly toxic to dogs — cycads (Zamia and relatives) are listed as toxic by ASPCA and are a leading cause of fatal plant poisoning in dogs. The seeds are especially deadly, causing liver failure. Keep pets well away and pick up any dropped seeds.

invasive.notes: Not invasive in Florida.

other_notes: The species epithet furfuracea is Latin for 'mealy' or 'scurfy,' describing the fine rusty fuzz that coats new growth and leaflets. The genus name Zamia derives from the Latin word for 'pine nut,' a reference to the cone-borne seeds shared by cycads and conifers.

Zamia furfuracea is listed as Endangered on the IUCN Red List, primarily due to decades of overcollection for the horticultural trade. Wild populations in Veracruz are now fragmented and small; nursery-propagated plants supply virtually all current commercial demand. Growing a legally sourced, nursery-propagated specimen contributes in a small way to reducing pressure on wild plants.

Observe and enjoy this plant safely. Because every part of the cardboard cycad is highly toxic — and the bright orange-red seeds are particularly conspicuous — please keep pets on a short leash and on established pathways when near this planting. If you are visiting with children, ask them to look rather than touch; the seeds should never be picked up or handled.
internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #372611510 (harrisonmello); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review.
species.watch_invasive_notes: UF/IFAS flags Zamia furfuracea as a caution species — it may be used in Florida landscapes but should be managed to prevent escape into natural areas. No FISC (formerly FLEPPC) Category I or II listing was found.

butterfly.notes: The Atala butterfly's primary and well-documented larval host is the native coontie (Zamia integrifolia); the Wikipedia account of Eumaeus atala mentions 'other exotic ornamental cycads' generically as supplemental hosts, but no source located explicitly names Z. furfuracea as a confirmed host plant, and at Bradenton's latitude the Atala is at the northern edge of its range and uncommon. Adult Atala butterflies do not use cycads as nectar sources.

safety_note: Every part of this plant is seriously toxic to people, dogs, cats, and horses — do not eat any part of it, and keep pets away from it entirely. The primary toxin is cycasin, a compound that causes liver failure; a second compound, beta-methylamino-L-alanine, causes neurological signs including seizures. The ASPCA notes that as few as one or two seeds can be fatal to a dog.

The bright orange-red seeds are the most dangerous part because they are attractive and contain the highest concentrations of toxin, but leaves, stem, and roots are also toxic. If a pet chews any part of this plant, contact a veterinarian or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center (888-426-4435) immediately.

quick_hits: The big, trumpet-shaped blooms on a tall hollow stalk are the classic "amaryllis" sold around the holidays — though botanically it is Hippeastrum.
Each flower can be six inches or more across, in vivid red, pink, white, or striped.
The whole plant grows from a large bulb, and that bulb is the most poisonous part.
It contains lycorine, the same toxin found in daffodils and crinum lilies.
origin: Native to tropical and subtropical South America. Bred extensively into the large-flowered hybrids sold worldwide, and grown as a garden bulb in warm climates including Florida.
more_information: The plants sold as "amaryllis," especially the showy holiday bulbs, are mostly Hippeastrum — a separate genus from the true amaryllis, though the common name has stuck for generations. From a single large bulb rises a tall, hollow stalk topped with two to several enormous trumpet-shaped flowers in red, pink, white, salmon, or candy-striped patterns.

The drama comes with a caution. Like its relatives in the amaryllis family — daffodils, crinums, spider lilies — it contains lycorine and related alkaloids, concentrated most heavily in the bulb. Eating it causes vomiting, drooling, and abdominal upset, and a large dose is more serious.
In Florida's mild climate the bulbs can stay in the ground year-round and multiply, returning to bloom each year rather than being treated as one-time holiday gifts.
wildlife_value: The large flowers can attract bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds when in bloom. Wildlife value is otherwise limited, and the toxic bulbs are not a safe food source.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: A bulb plant sending up strap leaves and a tall, hollow flower stalk.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Two to several very large, trumpet-shaped blooms atop a thick hollow stalk; red, pink, white, salmon, or striped.

blocks.label: Bulb
blocks.text: Large, rounded, the most toxic part.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Long, strap-shaped, glossy green, appearing with or after the flowers.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Huge trumpet flowers on a tall, leafless hollow stalk rising from strap-like leaves. The flower size alone is distinctive.

size.habit: clumping bulb

growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · rich, well-drained
growing_conditions.spacing: in Florida the bulbs can stay in the ground and naturalize, returning yearly

other_notes: Modern hybrid amaryllis bulbs are a major global trade commodity. The Netherlands, South Africa, Israel, and Brazil are the leading producers; the bulbs sold in Florida garden centers are typically Dutch or Israeli stock grown from cultivars developed over two-plus centuries of breeding.

The eastern lubber grasshopper (Romalea microptera) is noted by UF/IFAS as an occasional chewing pest on amaryllis in Florida. Handpicking is the recommended first response; the insects are large and easy to spot.
species.internal_notes: Added 2026-06-18 from iNaturalist gap analysis (June 2026 batch). Anchor obs #344005323 (jsea); Unique ID written to iNat. Shell row — content fields pending review. Genus-level ID — refine to species when possible.
safety_note: Keep dogs away from this plant — the ASPCA lists Hippeastrum as toxic to dogs, cats, and horses, with the bulb being the most dangerous part. Lycorine and related alkaloids can cause vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, excessive drooling, and in larger ingestions, tremors or cardiac effects. If a pet chews on any part of the plant, contact your veterinarian promptly.
People are generally at low risk from casual contact, but ingesting the bulb or other plant parts can cause stomach upset and should be avoided; this is an ornamental plant, not a food plant.

butterfly.notes: Hippeastrum is a documented larval host for two moth species in the Noctuidae family — the Spanish Moth (Xanthopastis timais), confirmed by UF/IFAS for Florida, and the Crinum Grub Moth (Spodoptera picta), noted in Wikipedia. No butterfly species (Rhopalocera) are documented as larval hosts, and no evidence was found establishing Hippeastrum as a notable adult nectar source for butterflies or moths.

quick_hits: Skyflower puts on a striking two-color show — delicate sky-blue to purple flower sprays and drooping clusters of bright golden-orange berries appear on the same plant at the same time.
Those cheerful golden berries are highly toxic to mammals, including dogs and cats — a serious catch hiding behind an appealing look.
The nectar-rich flowers are an exceptional, near year-round draw for Florida butterflies and hummingbirds, making this one of the garden's hardest-working pollinator shrubs.
The arching, sometimes thorny stems give the shrub a graceful, fountain-like weeping shape — beautiful, but occasionally biting.
origin: Native to the Caribbean, Mexico, and Central and South America, Skyflower thrives in the warmth of Florida's climate. It is widely cultivated as a fast-growing ornamental hedge or standalone specimen, valued for its fountain-like habit and virtually nonstop blooming cycle in our region.
more_information: Skyflower (Duranta erecta) is a favorite for its long bloom and lively color contrast. Loose, cascading sprays of small, five-lobed flowers in sky blue, lavender, or purple appear nearly year-round in Florida's warmth, and because flowering and fruiting overlap, a plant often displays flowers and shining golden-orange berries simultaneously.

Those berries carry a serious warning. Despite looking like edible fruit, the berries and leaves are toxic, containing saponins, iridoid glycosides, and other compounds; a historically recorded fatal poisoning of a child and documented severe poisonings in dogs and cats underscore the risk. It is a plant to enjoy with eyes, not mouths.

For wildlife it is a strong performer — the nectar-rich flowers are reliable butterfly and hummingbird draws — and its arching, sometimes lightly thorny stems give it a graceful, fountain-like shape that suits hedges and specimen plantings alike.
wildlife_value: The nectar-rich flowers are an exceptional, near year-round draw for butterflies and hummingbirds, making this one of the garden's better pollinator shrubs. Some songbirds can safely consume the berries that are toxic to mammals, but the plant's primary wildlife value is as a nectar source for pollinators.

blocks.label: Form
blocks.text: An arching, sometimes thorny evergreen shrub with a graceful, weeping habit.

blocks.label: Flowers
blocks.text: Loose, cascading sprays of small, five-lobed, sky-blue to purple flowers much of the year.

blocks.label: Fruit
blocks.text: Clusters of round, glossy, golden-orange drupes — attractive but toxic.

blocks.label: Leaves
blocks.text: Oval, light to medium green, opposite, sometimes finely toothed.

reproduction.what_to_look_for: Blue-purple flower sprays and golden berry clusters together on an arching shrub. The simultaneous flowers-and-berries display is the signature — and a cue to keep the berries away from children and pets.

size.habit: arching, weeping, sometimes thorny

growing_conditions.light: full sun to part shade (best bloom in sun)
growing_conditions.soil_tolerances: sand · loam · clay · acidic to slightly alkaline · well-drained
growing_conditions.spacing: frost-sensitive; stems may die back in rare severe freezes but typically rebound quickly; stems can carry thorns

detail: Not edible — the berries and leaves are poisonous despite the berries' appealing look. See toxicity for full hazard detail.
